reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: Clear, self-contained opening paragraph that answers 'what is this and why does it matter' without meta-framing.
The Notifications vs. Alarms vs. Budgets comparison table is the best section on the page — gives a first-time user exactly the mental model they need to decide where to go.
Good 'When to use' section with four concrete, relatable scenarios; also explicitly says when you DON'T need the feature.
FAQ mixes Stacktape-specific questions, behavior questions, and comparison questions well — the FAQ structure is strong.
No YAML Stacktape config blocks, no wrong TS patterns, no marketing language — all mandatory style rules pass.
Scoping section clearly explains project + stage scoping and their combination, which is non-obvious behavior the reader would otherwise have to discover by trial.
problems: The '17 event types organized into six categories' table with specific event names (Deploy started, Deploy succeeded, Deploy failed, Deploy rolled back, Delete started, etc.) is NOT grounded in the provided source files. The only provided source (NotificationsPage.tsx) shows event types as a string array but does not enumerate them. CreateNotificationModal — the component that would expose the options — is not in the source list. If any of those event type strings are wrong, the most important section of the page misleads users.

Delivery status table (Sent, Partial, Failed, Pending, No match) is entirely ungrounded in the provided source files. Neither NotificationsPage.tsx nor Explanations.tsx enumerates these status values. The notificationHistory explanation only says 'including their delivery status' without naming the statuses.
'Stacktape does not retry failed deliveries automatically' is an ungrounded behavioral claim — not mentioned anywhere in the provided source.

Navigation sub-paths 'Notifications > Rules' and 'Notifications > History' are not confirmed in the provided source. NotificationsPage.tsx shows a single page with a table — there is no separate 'Rules' sub-page visible in the source. Only the 'notificationHistory' key in Explanations.tsx hints that a history page exists, without confirming the path.

Terminology drift: the source table shows column headers 'For projects' and 'For stages' (from NotificationsPage.tsx), but the page describes the form fields as 'Active for projects' and 'Active for stages'. These are different labels for the same concepts.

The page references <ConsoleScreenshot> component with screenshot paths ('/static/screenshots/console-create-notification.png', etc.) that are not present in source — these screenshots may not exist and the component usage cannot be verified against any example page since none was provided.
mandatoryFixes: Ground the event types enumeration: either include CreateNotificationModal.tsx in the source and verify each event type string exactly, or replace the detailed table with a cautious statement like 'Event types cover deployment lifecycle events (deploys, deletes, rollbacks), GitOps pushes, script runs, and secret changes — select from the dropdown in the Console for the current full list.' Never enumerate 17 named event types if the source doesn't confirm them.

Remove or soften the delivery status table (Sent, Partial, Failed, Pending, No match) — these values are not present in any provided source file. Replace with the grounded claim from notificationHistory explanation: 'The history page shows a log of all notifications including their delivery status.' Do not invent specific status string values.
Remove the ungrounded behavioral claim 'Stacktape does not retry failed deliveries automatically' — this is not stated anywhere in the provided source.
Align terminology with the source: change 'Active for projects' / 'Active for stages' to 'For projects' / 'For stages' to match the column headers visible in NotificationsPage.tsx.
optionalImprovements: The page could note that notification rules can be deleted (a DeleteButton is visible in NotificationsPage.tsx source), giving first-time users confidence they can clean up test rules without consequence.

The FAQ answer to 'Can I create notification rules from the CLI?' is good but could also mention that API keys created in the Console authenticate the CLI — so the CLI triggers events, but rule management is Console-only. Helps users understand the CLI/Console boundary.

The 'How many notification rules can I create?' FAQ answer says 'no documented limit' — consider omitting this entry or rephrasing as 'There is no published limit' and pointing to Console for current quota information. 'No documented limit' in docs can read as uninformed.

Consider adding a brief mention that notification rules can be deleted, and that deleting a rule does not affect past notification history — this is implied by the source (DeleteButton component) and is useful operational context for a first-time user.

The <Tip> callout suggesting separate rules for different severity levels is useful, but it would land better if positioned after the scoping section rather than in the middle of the creation steps — it's more of a usage pattern than a creation-time tip.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: The 'Notifications vs. alarms vs. budgets' comparison table is exactly what production engineers need to decide which alert system to configure — it nails the use-case split and correctly notes that alarm rules require a redeploy while notification rules do not.
The scoping section (project + stage) is practical and concrete, with a clear statement of what happens when both are set simultaneously.

The FAQ entry distinguishing 'Do notification rules require a new deployment?' correctly cites the difference from alarm rules (grounded in the alarmRules explanation in Explanations.tsx) — this is the single most useful production-readiness fact on the page.
The 'When to use' section gives actionable scenarios (GitOps visibility, secret auditing, multi-team scoping) rather than restating the feature definition.
First paragraph is self-contained and answer-first per AEO guidelines — no meta-framing.
problems: The table of 17 event types in 6 named categories is a highly specific claim not grounded in any provided source file. NotificationsPage.tsx only shows event types rendered dynamically from trpc API data — it does not enumerate the specific names or confirm any count. The categorized breakdown (Deploy started/succeeded/failed/rolled back, Preview deploy succeeded/failed, etc.) appears to be invented rather than sourced.

The delivery status table (Sent, Partial, Failed, Pending, No match) and their behavioral descriptions are absent from all provided source files. The 'Notifications > History' Explanations.tsx entry only says 'A log of all notification alerts that were sent, including their delivery status' — it does not enumerate specific status values or their meanings.

The claim 'Stacktape does not retry failed deliveries automatically' is not grounded in any provided source. This is a concrete production behavior that could mislead engineers into not setting up redundant alerting.

The FAQ has 8 entries, all Stacktape-specific operational questions. The style guide requires mixing three categories: (1) Stacktape-specific, (2) AWS service mechanics, and (3) choosing/comparison questions. No entry asks 'When should I use notifications instead of alarms?' or 'How do notification delivery failures compare to CloudWatch alarm state transitions?' — the high-intent comparison questions are missing.

The three H3 subsections under 'Supported event types' (Deployment events, Git deployment events, Secret events) cover only 3 of the 6 table rows with prose, leaving Rollback, Scripts, and Secrets without equivalent treatment. This creates inconsistent depth and the subsections largely restate the table rather than adding decision-relevant context.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or heavily qualify the specific 17-event-type count and the categorized event types table. The provided source files (NotificationsPage.tsx, Explanations.tsx) confirm that event types exist as a dynamic list but do not enumerate specific names or a total count. Replace with what the source confirms: that event types span deploy, delete, rollback, script, and secret operations. Alternatively, state that the full list of available event types is visible in the Console when creating a rule — this is accurate and does not overcommit to a specific enumeration.

Remove or qualify the delivery status table (Sent, Partial, Failed, Pending, No match) and the behavioral claim 'Stacktape does not retry failed deliveries automatically'. Neither the status codes nor the retry behavior are present in any provided source file. Replace with: the Notifications > History page (confirmed by Explanations.tsx notificationHistory entry) shows delivery status for each event; direct readers to that page for troubleshooting without inventing specific status code names.

Add at least 2 FAQ entries covering either AWS service mechanics or choosing/comparison questions. Current FAQ is entirely Stacktape-operational, which fails the style guide's three-category requirement. Suitable additions: 'When should I use a notification rule instead of an alarm?' (comparison) and 'What happens to notifications when I delete a project or stage?' (operational edge case). These do not require ungrounded source claims.
optionalImprovements: Remove the three H3 event-type subsections (Deployment events, Git deployment events, Secret events) or give all categories equal treatment. As written, these subsections only restate the table with slightly more prose — they add little decision value and their uneven coverage makes the section feel incomplete.

The Tip about routing failures to high-urgency channels and successes to low-noise webhooks is useful but generic. Strengthen it with a concrete example: e.g., route 'Deploy failed' + 'Rollback failed' to a PagerDuty webhook, and 'Deploy succeeded' to a Slack #deploy-log channel.
Consider adding a note on whether notification history has a retention period or row limit — engineers setting up auditing for secret changes will want to know if the history is permanent or rolling.
The page uses both `<Info>` and `<Tip>` callout types. Since no example page was provided, this is acceptable, but confirm these are both standard in the docs component palette before publishing.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is answer-first, self-contained, and explains the purpose of notification rules clearly.
The page is easy to scan, with short H2 headings, a practical comparison table, and an FAQ that covers common operational questions.
The distinction between notifications and metric-based alarms is useful for the intended reader and aligns with the Console explanations at a high level.
The page avoids forbidden Stacktape config patterns, YAML config examples, bad shell blocks, and marketing language.
problems: Many concrete claims are not grounded in the provided sources. The source only shows the Notifications Console page table, create button, delete button, tooltip explanation, and general explanations; it does not provide the notification modal internals, event enum, delivery status enum, history table implementation, retry behavior, or notification routing semantics.
The 17-event table is unsupported by the provided source files. None of the listed event names, categories, or the exact count appear in the source.

The Console workflow is over-specified. Claims such as `Notifications > Rules`, grouped dropdowns, default all-project/all-stage behavior in the modal, expandable delivery details, and screenshot-specific UI details are not present in the provided source files.

Several FAQ answers assert unsupported behavior: Console-only management, no CLI/config-file support, multiple matching rule fan-out, immediate effect without deploy, no automatic retry, and exact semantics of `Deploy failed` versus `Deploy rolled back`.

The page claims notification delivery statuses `Sent`, `Partial`, `Failed`, `Pending`, and `No match`, but the provided source only says notification history includes delivery status. The status values and meanings are not grounded.
The page says secret events fire when secrets are changed through the Console or CLI, but the provided sources do not show secret notification event types or CLI behavior.

The page says all three systems deliver through the same alert channels and appear in unified alert history. Alert channels and unified alert history are generally grounded by Explanations, but the exact relationship for notifications, alarms, and budgets is broader than the provided notification source proves.

The FAQ is useful but Stacktape-specific only. It does not include broader choosing/comparison or underlying AWS-service questions, though this page is a Console observability page where fewer AWS-service FAQs may be acceptable.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or source-ground the 17 supported event types. If the event enum is required, add the source file that defines notification event types and revise the table to match it exactly.

Remove or source-ground delivery status values and retry behavior. If status meanings matter, add the source file that defines notification history records or delivery statuses, then document only those values.

Replace unsupported Console navigation and modal claims with narrower wording grounded in `NotificationsPage.tsx`, or add `CreateNotificationModal` and notification history page sources before documenting fields, defaults, grouped dropdowns, and expandable delivery details.

Revise FAQ answers that make unsupported negative or lifecycle claims. In particular, do not state there is no CLI/config property, no automatic retry, immediate effect, or multi-rule fan-out unless those behaviors are grounded in source.

Delete or qualify the screenshot-specific assertions unless the screenshot files or Console components are part of the source set. The provided source does not prove the modal and history screenshot contents.
optionalImprovements: Add a short source-grounded section explaining what the visible Notifications page actually shows: event types, projects, stages, destination channel, creation time, and deletion action.
Keep the notifications-versus-alarms-versus-budgets comparison, but narrow it to what the Explanations source supports and avoid claiming implementation details not shown here.

If additional sources are available, include `CreateNotificationModal`, notification history page/components, server notification types, and any API/router definitions; those would allow a much more useful and precise page.
Add one or two comparison FAQ entries, such as when to use notifications versus alarms, using only the grounded distinction between operational events and metric thresholds.

verifierResults.summary: The draft over-specifies the Notifications feature far beyond the provided Console source. The source supports notification rules with event types, project/stage scoping, alert channels, creation, deletion, and a separate notification-history explanation, but it does not support the detailed event catalog, delivery statuses, retry behavior, field defaults, screenshots, or several Console navigation claims.

issues.reason: The provided source only says notification rules watch specific events such as deployment succeeding, failing, or being canceled. It does not enumerate 17 event types, categories, GitOps events, script events, secret events, delete events, rollback events, or the exact display labels in the draft.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the page to the supported source facts: notification rules watch selected event types and deliver to a channel. If event names must be listed, use only event names present in the provided source or add the backend enum/source file that defines the notification event catalog.

issues.reason: The provided source only says notification history includes delivery status. It does not define the possible statuses or their meanings.

issues.statement: "Stacktape does not retry failed deliveries automatically."
issues.reason: No provided source describes retry behavior for failed notification deliveries. This claim affects troubleshooting expectations and should not be asserted without backend delivery source.

evidence.quote: Check here if you're not sure whether a notification was delivered.

issues.statement: "All three deliver through the same alert channels" for notifications, alarms, and budgets.

evidence.quote: Create channels here, then use them in notification rules, alarm rules, and budget alerts to receive alerts where you need them.

issues.statement: "Notification rules take effect immediately when created. They watch for events at the organization level, not at the AWS resource level."
issues.reason: The source shows notification rules are queried by organization and created in the Console, but it does not state immediate effect or the implementation boundary between organization-level and AWS-resource-level event handling.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the contrast only where sourced: alarm rule infrastructure changes happen on the next deployment. For notifications, say they are managed in the Console, without claiming immediate effect unless backend source supports it.

evidence.quote: Creating or changing a rule saves it in the Console. Stacktape creates, updates, or removes the actual CloudWatch alarms and EventBridge notification routing the next time each matching project and stage is deployed.

With no primary config type and no CreateNotificationModal or history page source included, the safest audit findings are about unsupported Console workflow/details rather than missing API properties.

issues.statement: Do notification rules require a new deployment to take effect? No. Unlike alarm rules, notification rules take effect immediately when created.
issues.reason: The source supports the contrast that alarm rules are applied on the next deployment, but it does not state when notification rules take effect. Immediate effect is a Stacktape-specific behavior claim that is not grounded in the provided notification sources.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the alarm contrast only if useful, but avoid the immediacy claim unless backend or modal source confirms it. For example: "Alarm rule infrastructure changes are applied on the next deployment; the provided notification source does not describe a deployment step for notification rules."

evidence.quote: Creating or changing a rule saves it in the Console. Stacktape creates, updates, or removes the actual CloudWatch alarms and EventBridge notification routing the next time each matching project and stage is deployed.

positiveFindings: The page correctly separates operational notifications from metric-based alarms, which is consistent with the Console explanations for notifications and alarm rules.
The draft mentions alert channels as shared destinations for notification rules, alarm rules, and budget alerts, matching the alert channel explanation source.
The page avoids forbidden Stacktape config example patterns; no hand-authored YAML, plain-object StacktapeConfig, or fenced Stacktape TypeScript config appears in the draft.

reviewerResults.persona: Developer trying Stacktape for the first time. Low AWS experience. Wants to copy-paste a working example and ship something, then come back to the page when they need to extend it. Cares about: a clear path from "what is this" to "I have it running", absence of unexplained AWS jargon, a runnable example near the top, and confidence that the page is telling them everything they need to start. Does NOT care about exhaustive option coverage — that's what the API reference is for.
strengths: No forbidden config patterns (YAML blocks, StacktapeConfig type, fenced TypeScript with defineConfig) — correct for a Console-only feature with no stacktape.ts knobs.

The column-by-column table in 'Managing notification rules' is directly grounded in the NotificationsPage.tsx source (columnHeaders array matches exactly) and gives a first-time reader an accurate mental model before they open the Console.

The three-row comparison table (Notifications vs Alarms vs Budgets) is the single most useful piece on the page — concrete, scannable, and decision-supporting. A new user can immediately see which tool to reach for.
When-to-use and When-NOT-to-use are peer H2 sections as required — not nested.

FAQ covers all three required categories: Stacktape-specific ('Can I create rules from the CLI?'), use-case ('How do I know if a notification was delivered?'), and comparison ('What is the difference between notifications and alarms?'). Nine Q&A pairs is a good count for a narrow-scope page.
Alert channel prerequisite is called out before the creation workflow ('You must have at least one alert channel configured'), saving the reader a frustrating dead-end.
The scoping section correctly explains combinability of project + stage scoping with a concrete example (project 'api', stage 'production').
problems: The 'Event types' section is deliberately vague — it tells the reader to check the Console for the full list without naming even the high-level categories. The Explanations.tsx source mentions 'deployment succeeding, failing, or being canceled' and the page's own prose mentions 'deploys, deletes, rollbacks, and cancellations'. That coverage already exists in the opening paragraph; the dedicated section adds almost nothing and instead redirects to the Console for information that's partially available in the source. This reads as a placeholder rather than a guide.

The Notification history section (its own H2) is thin enough to border on a padded backbone section. It restates the explanations.tsx tooltip almost verbatim ('A log of all notification alerts that were sent, including their delivery status. Check here if you're not sure whether a notification was delivered.') and adds only a single troubleshooting tip. It does not tell the reader WHERE in the Console to find the history page (route, navigation path), which is the one thing a first-time reader needs.

The FAQ cost answer ('The cost is part of your Stacktape subscription. Stacktape does not charge per notification event.') makes a Stacktape business/pricing claim that does not appear in either source file. This is speculative and may be wrong or outdated. Pricing guidance rules require source grounding for Stacktape-specific claims.

The <ConsoleScreenshot> MDX component is used but is not listed in the style guide's component palette and does not appear in any inline source. If this component is not defined in the docs platform it will silently fail to render. Its validity should be confirmed before publication.

The 'Event types' section uses a <Tip> callout that says 'the full list of available event types is visible in the Console when creating a notification rule' — this is accurate but unhelpful. A reader who just read this section still doesn't know whether there are 3 event types or 30, or whether they cover things like 'deployment script failure' or 'package workloads'. The tip adds no information the surrounding prose didn't already imply.
mandatoryFixes: Notification history section: either expand it to tell the reader where in the Console to find the history (navigation path grounded in the source), or merge its content into the FAQ entries that already cover it (FAQ Q3 and Q8 overlap substantially with this section). As written, it is close to a padded backbone section that restates the tooltip without adding guidance.

FAQ cost answer: remove or heavily qualify the claim that notifications are 'part of your Stacktape subscription' and 'Stacktape does not charge per notification event'. Neither statement is grounded in the provided source files. Replace with a cautious framing such as 'Notification rules themselves do not create AWS-billed resources; costs come from the downstream alert channels (Slack, email infrastructure, webhook endpoints). See your Stacktape plan for platform feature coverage.' Do not invent subscription pricing.

Event types section: either enumerate the known high-level event categories (deploy success/failure, delete, rollback, cancel — all of which appear in the page's own prose already) or collapse this section into the opening paragraph and remove the H2. A standalone H2 section whose only actionable content is 'see the Console' does not meet the section-depth requirement.
optionalImprovements: Verify that <ConsoleScreenshot> is a registered MDX component in the docs platform before publishing. If it is not, replace with a standard Markdown image or remove the screenshot reference.

The opening paragraph is strong and self-contained (good AEO posture), but the phrase 'and more' after listing event types is vague. Replace with a concrete count or a parenthetical ('and related cancellation events') so AI extractors have a precise fact to quote.

Consider adding a one-sentence note in the 'Managing notification rules' section about what happens if you delete an alert channel that a notification rule references — a first-time reader setting up a channel might wonder about this dependency direction.

The <Tip> in the 'Scoping notifications' section (about creating separate rules for different severity levels) is genuinely useful guidance that would read better as regular prose — callout boxes used for routine best-practice tips lose their visual weight. Reserve <Tip> for non-obvious gotchas.

The comparison table's 'Defined in' column uses the phrasing 'Console — applied on next deploy' for alarms, which is grounded in Explanations.tsx. Consider adding an equivalent note for budgets ('Console — organization-wide alerts apply immediately; stack-scoped alerts apply on next deploy') to make all three rows equally precise.

reviewerResults.persona: Engineer already shipping with Stacktape, looking up how to configure something correctly under real conditions. Cares about: accuracy of defaults, limits, and behavior under load; whether edge cases and failure modes are mentioned where relevant (scaling, security defaults, cost ceilings, rollback behavior); whether the page would mislead them when they reach production. Does NOT nitpick prose polish or MDX component choices — only flags things that change what they'd configure.
strengths: The comparison table (Notifications vs Alarms vs Budgets) is the strongest part of the page — it answers the most common decision question in a single scannable view.
Scoping section is precise and grounded in source: `allLabel="All projects"` / `allLabel="All stages"` behavior is correctly described, project+stage AND-combination is stated explicitly.
Clear separation between CLI role (fires events) and Console role (manages rules) — satisfies the observability section instruction to never blur the two.
Info callout at the top correctly redirects metric-based alerting to /observability/alarms before the reader wastes time — good answer-first structure.
FAQ mixes all three required categories well: Stacktape-specific (CLI vs Console), use-case (how to set up multi-team), and comparison (notifications vs alarms vs budgets).
"When NOT to use" is non-circular and concrete: manual deployments, runtime health monitoring are named as genuine exclusions.
problems: The body prose states 'Channels are the destinations (Slack, Microsoft Teams, Discord, Email, or Webhook)' — Microsoft Teams and Discord are not confirmed in the provided source files. `Explanations.tsx` only mentions 'a Slack channel, an email address, or a webhook' as examples. The seoDescription frontmatter also enumerates 'Slack, Teams, Email, Discord, or Webhook', same sourcing concern.

<ConsoleScreenshot src="/static/screenshots/console-notifications-page.png" ...> references a screenshot path and a component not confirmed in the provided source files. If the component or path does not exist, this renders as a broken block on the live page.

Event types section says 'select from the available event types shown in the creation dialog' without naming a single concrete type, then punts to 'The full list is visible in the Console'. The source (NotificationsPage.tsx) shows event types as string arrays with sentenceCase display but does not enumerate values — this is correctly hedged, but the Tip callout repeats the same non-information twice. The second mention adds no value.

The 'Notification history' section is described as if it is a tab or view on the same Notifications page, but Explanations.tsx has a distinct `notificationHistory` entry suggesting it may be a separate Console page. The draft never clarifies the navigation path to this history view.
mandatoryFixes: No hard style violations (no YAML config blocks, no StacktapeConfig type, no fenced TS blocks with defineConfig, no marketing language, no multi-command bash blocks) were found. No mandatory fixes required.
optionalImprovements: Narrow the channel type enumeration in body prose to what the provided source confirms ('Slack, email, or webhook'), and add 'see Alert Channels for the full list of supported destinations' rather than listing Teams and Discord without source backing.

Remove or verify the <ConsoleScreenshot> component — if the component is valid in this docs system, keep it; if not, replace the block with a prose sentence describing the table layout (which you already have in the preceding paragraph, making the screenshot largely redundant).

Cut or merge the redundant Tip callout in the Event types section ('The full list of available event types is visible in the Console...') — the paragraph directly above it already says the same thing. Two sentences making the same non-claim in the same section wastes the reader's attention.

Clarify whether notification history is on the same Notifications page or a separate Console route — Explanations.tsx has a distinct `notificationHistory` key, which suggests a separate page. If it is separate, name the navigation path ('Notifications → History tab' or '/notifications/history') so the reader can find it.

The FAQ answer for 'How much do notifications cost?' is accurate but slightly hedged — it could note that the underlying alert delivery (e.g. Slack API, SMTP) has no AWS cost component since delivery is handled by the channel endpoint, not by an AWS service, making this genuinely a $0 AWS line item.

reviewerResults.persona:
AI answer engine, search index, or coding assistant reading this page to cite from it or extract a paragraph as a snippet. This reviewer combines what was previously two lenses (LLM consumer + SEO/AEO) and is the only persona judging extractability. Hard criteria — penalize each missing item by at least 1 point in the most relevant category:
1. **Answer-first paragraph.** The first paragraph (40-75 words) must answer 'what is this and why does it matter' in self-contained prose. No 'this guide will cover...' framing. AI Overviews extract this verbatim.
2. **Self-contained sections.** Every H2 section, when extracted alone, must make sense — its first paragraph defines the concept being discussed (e.g. 'A Stacktape web service can front traffic through CloudFront to cache...' not 'You can also enable a CDN'). Pronouns like 'this', 'it', 'the resource' that depend on surrounding context score worse.
3. **Explicit entity naming.** Say 'Stacktape web service' or 'a web service' — never just 'the service' or 'this resource'. Named entities anchor citations.
4. **Fact-dense, concrete.** Numbers, defaults, limits, supported values written as literals. 'Up to 16 vCPU and 120 GB memory' beats 'large resource limits'. Vague qualifiers ('fast', 'flexible', 'easy') are penalized.
5. **Tables for comparisons.** When the page compares options (engines, sizes, modes, packaging types), a table is preferred over prose — tables get cited 2-4x more often.
6. **Complete code examples.** No `<your-here>` / `// fill in` / `...` placeholders inside example bodies. The example must be copy-paste runnable as shown.
7. **Compact, descriptive headings.** Short noun-form H2s (Scaling, Networking) for navigation. Question-form H3s are allowed and welcome for FAQ-style subsections and common-question anchors. Avoid full-sentence H2s.
8. **FAQ section, broad scope.** Resource, reference, and concept pages SHOULD have an FAQ section, aiming for 8-10 Q&A pairs but accepting fewer when the page's scope doesn't yield that many distinct, useful questions. A narrow-scope page might have 4-6 strong FAQs; that's fine — quality over quota. Mix (a) Stacktape-specific questions, (b) use-case questions about the underlying AWS service (e.g. 'How much does ECS Fargate cost?'), and (c) choosing/comparison questions ('Web service vs Lambda?'). Penalize a Stacktape-only FAQ (it should cover what readers actually search for), and penalize FAQs padded with synthetic or near-duplicate questions just to hit a number. Do NOT penalize a smaller FAQ when the page's scope justifies it; do NOT penalize FAQ absence on pure narrative pages (Getting Started steps).
9. **Meta description suitability.** A clean 150-character summary should be derivable directly from the opening paragraph and frontmatter.
10. **No stripped pronouns / antecedent gaps.** A paragraph starting with 'This is configured by...' fails. Extracted paragraphs lose surrounding context — every paragraph should name what it's about up front.
11. **Union-type coverage discipline.** When a section documents a property that has multiple variants in the source's union type (packaging modes, compute engines, load balancer kinds, database engines, deployment strategies, trigger sources, etc.), all variants must be named somewhere on the page — not just the one being deep-dived. A page that has '### Using a custom Dockerfile' under Packaging without mentioning the four other packaging modes is misleading. Penalize heavily.
12. **Per-variant equal depth.** When a section covers multiple variants, each must be developed to roughly equivalent depth: code example per variant, tradeoffs per variant, cost/constraint context per variant. Enumeration without follow-through is not documentation. Example: a 'Compute resources' section that gives Fargate an example + cost table while EC2 gets two sentences fails this criterion.
13. **Decision support for opt-in features.** Sections introducing opt-ins (CDN, custom domains, gradual deployments, side containers, firewall, ARM, EC2 mode, warm pools, etc.) must help the reader DECIDE, not just CONFIGURE. Look for: when-to-enable (concrete scenario), when-default-suffices, cost/tradeoff signal, and an opinionated recommendation. A section that jumps from a 1-line definition to a code example without addressing these is a recipe, not a guide. Penalize as missing completeness AND practicalUsefulness.
14. **Unexplained tunable values.** Code examples that set specific tunable values (\`cloudfrontPriceClass: 'PriceClass_100'\`, \`instanceTypes: ['t3.medium']\`, \`enableWarmPool: true\`) need 1-2 sentences of prose explaining what the property controls and what the alternatives are. Otherwise the value reads as magic or required when it isn't.
15. **Section depth — AEO is about the first paragraph only.** The 40-75 word self-contained-snippet rule applies to the FIRST paragraph of each section. The rest of the section must develop the topic — examples, tradeoffs, cost, edge cases. Sections that consist of one short paragraph + one code example with no surrounding context are too brief and fail completeness, even if they look 'scannable'.
strengths: Opening paragraph is self-contained, concrete, and within the target snippet length.
The page uses short H2 headings, clear tables, and a practical comparison between notifications, alarms, and budgets.
Console table columns and the Create new notification button are grounded in the provided NotificationsPage source.
The page correctly distinguishes deployment-event notifications from metric-based alarms in several places.
problems: Several Stacktape-specific claims are not grounded in the provided source files: Microsoft Teams and Discord alert channels, rollback/delete/script event categories, GitOps notification behavior, notification history failure causes, and the claim that notification rules are Console-only with no CLI or config support.
The page includes pricing/cost claims for notifications even though the observability instructions say cost is a separate section and the provided source files contain no notification pricing facts.

The Event types section relies on unsupported detail. The source only gives examples of deployment succeeding, failing, or being canceled, plus an eventTypes field in the table; it does not provide the full event taxonomy or creation-dialog options.
The FAQ is useful, but several answers go beyond the source, especially multiple-rule matching behavior, CLI/config absence, production-stage filtering semantics, and per-notification pricing.
Some Console workflow details are asserted from unavailable modal/history source, such as requiring an alert channel before creating a rule and editing/fixing failed delivery causes.
The page is strong for AEO and scanning, but source-grounding risk is high because it turns limited Console source into broader product behavior.
mandatoryFixes: Remove or source-ground all unsupported channel types. With the provided source, say alert channels include examples such as Slack, email, or webhook; do not list Microsoft Teams or Discord unless the alert-channel source is provided.

Rewrite Event types to only state what the source supports: notification rules watch selected events, with examples of deployments succeeding, failing, or being canceled. Do not claim delete, rollback, script, or related operation categories unless sourced.
Remove the pricing FAQ or replace it with a link to cost documentation without claiming there is no per-notification AWS charge or that cost is part of the Stacktape subscription.
Remove or qualify the unsupported negative claim that notification rules cannot be created from CLI or config. The provided source only shows Console management, not absence of CLI/config support.
Remove unsupported GitOps-specific notification behavior and multiple-rule matching behavior unless source files for those flows are added.

Do not describe CreateNotificationModal behavior beyond the button opening it unless the modal source is provided; specifically remove the claim that an alert channel is required before creating a rule unless sourced.
optionalImprovements: Add a short source-grounded note that the Notifications table shows only the first two event labels and a '+N more' indicator when a rule watches more event types, since this is directly visible in the source.
Tighten the FAQ to 5-7 highly grounded questions instead of keeping broad answers that require unsupported backend behavior.
Make the comparison table more cautious: keep shared alert channels and alert history because Explanations.tsx supports them, but avoid lifecycle timing claims not present in source.
Replace 'and more' in the opening paragraph with a narrower phrase such as 'such as deployments succeeding, failing, or being canceled' to match the tooltip source.

verifierResults.summary: The draft is broadly aligned with the Console notification page for basic rule management, table columns, alert channels, and notification history. The main factual risk is that it expands notification event coverage and rule behavior beyond the provided source, especially around deletes, rollbacks, scripts, matching semantics, and pricing.

issues.reason: The provided notification source only supports alerts for specific events such as deployment succeeding, failing, or being canceled. It does not show deletes, rollbacks, scripts, or related operations as notification event types. Those may exist elsewhere, but they are not grounded in the provided source files.

issues.reason: The provided sources do not show notification matching or dispatch behavior. Multiple rules are listed in the Console table, but duplicate matching and independent delivery semantics are not visible in the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove the FAQ entry or phrase it cautiously: “The provided Console source shows multiple notification rules can exist; matching and delivery behavior is handled by Stacktape’s notification system.”

positiveFindings: The Notifications page table columns in the draft match the Console source: Event types, For projects, For stages, Sent to, Created, and a delete action column.
The draft correctly states that alert channels are shared by notification rules, alarm rules, and budget alerts, matching the channels explanation source.

The draft correctly distinguishes notification rules from alarm rules at a high level: notifications watch specific events, while alarm rules monitor AWS metrics and send alerts when thresholds are crossed.
All checked markdown cross-links point to routes present in the provided documentation navigation index.

verifierResults.summary: API-completeness checks are mostly not applicable because this page has no primary config type, no Stacktape config examples, and no documented union-typed API property in the provided sources. I found several source-grounding risks in the draft's Console-specific and lifecycle claims, but no CodeBlock demonstrativeness or union-variant completeness issues.

issues.statement: "Notification rules are managed exclusively in the Stacktape Console. There is no CLI command or `stacktape.ts` property for notification rules."
issues.reason: The provided Console source proves that notification rules are displayed, created, and deleted from the Notifications page, but it does not prove exclusivity or the absence of CLI/config support. The route has no primary typeName, so there is also no config source proving the negative claim.
issues.suggestedFix: Narrow the wording to what the source proves: "The provided Console page manages notification rules from the Stacktape Console." Avoid claiming no CLI or config support unless CLI/config sources are included.

issues.statement: "Notification rules alert your team when deployment lifecycle events occur — deploys, deletes, rollbacks, and more."
issues.reason: The tooltip source mentions deployment succeeding, failing, or being canceled. The Notifications page source stores arbitrary `eventTypes`, but the provided files do not enumerate deletes, rollbacks, scripts, or other operation families.
issues.suggestedFix: Use the grounded examples from `Explanations.tsx`: "Notification rules alert your team when selected events happen, such as a deployment succeeding, failing, or being canceled."

issues.statement: "You must have at least one alert channel configured before creating a notification rule."
issues.reason: The provided source proves each listed notification has an `alertChannel` and that `CreateNotificationModal` exists, but the modal implementation is not included. The source does not show a precondition requiring an existing alert channel before rule creation.
issues.suggestedFix: Say only that notification rules deliver to an alert channel, and link to alert channels. If the modal source is added and proves the requirement, keep the stronger wording.

issues.statement: "Notification history shows a log of all notification alerts that were sent, including their delivery status."
issues.reason: This fact is grounded in `Explanations.tsx`, but the draft also describes troubleshooting outcomes and delivery failure causes like expired Slack tokens and changed webhook URLs. Those causes are not present in the provided source files.
issues.suggestedFix: Keep the grounded history description, but remove or soften the specific failure-cause list unless alert-channel or notification-history implementation sources are provided.

evidence.quote: A log of all notification alerts that were sent, including their delivery status.

issues.statement: "Notifications and budgets take effect without a deployment."
issues.reason: The provided sources support that alarm rules are applied on the next deployment, and budget explanations mention organization-wide and stack-scoped budget behavior. They do not state that notification rules take effect immediately or without deployment.
issues.suggestedFix: Split the grounded facts: "Alarm rule infrastructure changes are applied on the next deployment. Notification rules are managed in the Console." Avoid the immediate-effect claim unless notification backend sources are included.

evidence.quote: Creating or changing a rule saves it in the Console. Stacktape creates, updates, or removes the actual CloudWatch alarms and EventBridge notification routing the next time each matching project and stage is deployed.
Notification rules let you get alerted when specific events happen — like a deployment succeeding, failing, or being canceled.

issues.statement: "Can one event trigger multiple notification rules? Yes."
issues.reason: The matching semantics for multiple notification rules are not visible in the provided Console page or explanations. The source proves rules have event types, project scopes, stage scopes, and alert channels, but not whether overlapping rules all deliver independently.
issues.suggestedFix: Remove this FAQ entry or replace it with a grounded answer about what the Notifications table shows: event types, project scope, stage scope, destination channel, and creation time.
